narration.default: Last tutorial's game ended on one guess — right or wrong, the game was over. Real games don't work that way. Today you'll build a version that gives the kid a second try if they miss the first time. We'll do it by NESTING one if inside another else — a little decision tree inside the bigger decision.

Type these lines — same opening as last time:

print("Guess the number (2 chances)!")

number = 5

guess = int(input("Enter your guess: "))

The title mentions the 2 chances up front so the kid knows what to expect. number is still 5. The first guess is taken exactly like before.

steps.title: The outer if — got it first try?

narration.default:
Type:

if guess == number:
    print("Correct!")

This is the "got it first try" path. If the kid guessed 5, Python prints the celebration and skips everything else. The interesting work happens in the else branch — when the first guess was wrong.

steps.title: The else — start the second chance

narration.default:
Type:

else:
    print("Try again")

    guess = int(input("Second chance: "))

Note the BLANK line between the two indented lines — that's for readability, Python doesn't mind. The else block first tells the kid they missed, then asks for another guess. The variable guess gets reassigned — the new answer overwrites the old one in the same box.

steps.title: The inner if — nested inside else
Run — win on the first try

narration.default: Click Run. Type 5 straight away. Terminal prints Correct! and stops. Python never entered the else — the second-chance code sat there untouched. That's how branches work: only the path you take runs.

Run again. Type 3. Terminal says Try again and asks Second chance. Type 5 this time. Terminal prints Correct! The outer if was false, so the else ran — it prompted, got a new guess, and the INNER if caught it. Two tries, one win.

Run once more. Type 3, then 7. Terminal: Try again, then Game over. The outer if missed, the else ran, the inner if missed too, the inner else was the final catch. Two chances, both wrong — game over. The shape of your code controls exactly how the game feels.

steps.title: Why we'll replace this with a loop next

narration.default: Copy-pasting the same code for two tries works — but imagine writing it for 10 tries, or "keep going until they win". You'd run out of room fast. Next tutorial introduces the while loop: a way to repeat the same block of code until a condition stops being true. Your game will suddenly accept unlimited tries in just a few lines.
